annotations.text: the loss of weight makes me consider the lesion a cancer until it has been proved otherwise.
The differential diagnosis of the hepatic lesion is long, because the imaging characteristics are not clear-cut.
Other benign lesions that should be considered in this case are focal nodular hyperplasia and hepatic adenoma.
if the lesion proved to be a locally advanced gastric lesion, the radiologic findings suggest that it could have been resected en bloc with the left lateral segment of the liver.
The absence of an appropriate history and the presence of a scar or necrosis on images of this patient's lesion suggest that it was not a hepatic adenoma.
These characteristics are reminiscent of a hemangioma, perhaps a cavernous hemangioma.
The tumor may become secondarily infected, giving rise to episodic fever, as occurred in this patient.
Among malignant tumors, hepatocellular carcinoma, its fibrolamellar variant, peripheral cholangiocarcinoma, angiosarcoma, and primary hepatic lymphoma are the most likely possibilities.
The absence of any evidence of these conditions in the patient under discussion and the appearance of the images make this diagnosis unlikely.
The description of the skin lesions suggests psoriasis, which is often confined to the hands and elbows and is characterized by scaling plaques.
Since the patient under discussion had no history of taking home remedies for her skin lesions, however, it is unlikely that she had been exposed to arsenic.

Complete resection is possible in 48 to 75 percent of patients with this type of hepatocellular carcinoma, as compared with a resectability rate of approximately 20 percent among patients with other types.
fibrolamellar carcinoma occurs most often in young patients, predominantly females. It is often slow-growing, and a cure is possible if the lesion is widely resected.

The microscopical features described in this case clearly distinguish the tumor from focal nodular hyperplasia, in which bile ducts are often prominent, the hepatocytes are small and regular, and mitotic figures are infrequent.
